subject: [PATCH 0/4] arm64/mm: contpte-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages

reviews.author: David Hildenbrand
reviews.summary: Identified a double-put bug in fuse_try_move_page() where the folio refcount drops to zero prematurely. Suggested adding an extra folio_get() before replace_page_cache_folio() to maintain the reference.

2026-03-10.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues with contpte-sized exec folios on arm64, specifically for 16K and 64K pages. It fixes the calculation of optimal contpte order in exec_folio_order() and modifies the mmap_miss heuristic to enable readahead for executable memory. Additionally, it ensures that virtual addresses are aligned to CONT_PTE_SIZE for contpte mapping.

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary: Raised questions about the memory pressure implications of the proposed changes, suggesting that they might not be worse than existing x86 behavior. Also mentioned an alternative approach using dynamic linker patches.

2026-03-13.patch_summary: The patch series proposes to introduce contpt-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages on arm64, addressing memory pressure concerns.

reviews.author: hev
reviews.summary: Reviewer hev ran benchmarks on RK3399 and observed a significant reduction in iTLB misses after applying the patch, but questioned whether it would be better to align the virtual address to PMD size instead of CONT_PTE_SIZE.
reviews.raw_body:
From: WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

Hi,

I ran a quick bench on RK3399:

Binutils: 2.46
GCC: 15.2.1 (--enable-host-pie)

Workload: building vmlinux from Linux v7.0-rc1 with allnoconfig.

                Base                 Patchset [1]         Patchset [2]
instructions    3,115,852,636,773    3,194,533,947,809    3,235,417,205,947
cpu-cycles      8,374,429,970,450    8,457,398,871,141    8,323,881,987,768
itlb-misses         9,250,336,037        8,033,415,293        2,946,152,935
                                             ~ -13.16%            ~ -68.15%
time elapsed             610.51 s             605.12 s             593.83 s

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260310145406.3073394-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260313005211.882831-1-r@hev.cc

Should we prefer to PMD aligned here?

Thanks,
Rui

reviews.author: hev
reviews.summary: The reviewer suggests moving the logic for determining whether a virtual address needs to be aligned to an exec folio size from the current location in the code to a separate function, maximum_alignment(), or even better, a new helper function should_align_to_exec_folio().
reviews.raw_body:
I think this logic should live in maximum_alignment(), so we don't
have to walk the segments twice. It might be better to move it into a
separate helper, something like should_align_to_exec_folio()?

reviews.author: WANG Rui
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that even when a user-space shared library's code segment satisfies PMD_SIZE (32MB), it may not end up at a PMD-aligned virtual address, and suggested skipping the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case to avoid calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same parameters.
reviews.raw_body:
I noticed that even when the code segment of a user-space shared library
satisfies PMD_SIZE (32MB), it still doesn\u2019t end up at a PMD-aligned virtual
address. This might be the fallback you mentioned. Adjusting p_align in the
ld.so ELF loader does work, though it also avoids extremely large PMD_SIZE
values (capped at \u226432M).

It would probably be better to skip the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case here,
so we don\u2019t end up calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same
parameters.

Thanks,
Rui

reviews.author: WANG Rui
reviews.summary: Reviewer WANG Rui pointed out that the original patch's focus was on 64K normal pages, but they were actually referring to AArch64 with 4K normal pages. They provided measurement results showing that aligning code to PMD size (2M) performs slightly better than 64K for 4K pages.
reviews.raw_body:
I only just realized your focus was on 64K normal pages, what I was
referring to here is AArch64 with 4K normal pages.

Sorry about the earlier numbers. They were a bit low precision.
RK3399 has pretty limited PMU events, and it looks like it can\u2019t
collect events from the A53 and A72 clusters at the same time, so
I reran the measurements on the A53.

Even though the A53 backend isn\u2019t very wide, we can still see the
impact from iTLB pressure. With 4K pages, aligning the code to PMD
size (2M) performs slightly better than 64K.

Binutils: 2.46
GCC: 15.2.1 (--enable-host-pie)

Workload: building vmlinux from Linux v7.0-rc1 with allnoconfig.
Loop: 5

                Base                 Patchset [1]         Patchset [2]
instructions    1,994,512,163,037    1,994,528,896,322    1,994,536,148,574
cpu-cycles      6,890,054,789,351    6,870,685,379,047    6,720,442,248,967
                                              ~ -0.28%             ~ -2.46%
itlb-misses           579,692,117          455,848,211           43,814,795
                                             ~ -21.36%            ~ -92.44%
time elapsed            1331.15 s            1325.50 s            1296.35 s
                                              ~ -0.42%             ~ -2.61%

Maybe we could make exec_folio_order() choose differently folio size
depending on the configuration and conditional in some way, for example
based on the size of the code segment?

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260310145406.3073394-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260313005211.882831-1-r@hev.cc

Thanks,
Rui

reviews.author: WANG Rui
reviews.summary: Reviewer WANG Rui noted that most AArch64 and LoongArch64 binaries place the executable segment in the first PT_LOAD, satisfying alignment requirements for PMD-sized or large folio mappings, but this is not true for x86 due to linker defaults, specifically -z separate-code being the default on x86.
reviews.raw_body:
On Arch Linux for AArch64 and LoongArch64 I've observed that most
binaries place the executable segment in the first PT_LOAD. In that
case both the virtual address and file offset are 0, which happens to
satisfy the alignment requirements for PMD-sized or large folio
mappings.

x86 looks quite different. The executable segment is usually not the
first one.

After digging into this I realized this mostly comes from the linker
defaults. With GNU ld, -z noseparate-code merges the read-only and
read-only-executable segments into one, while -z separate-code
splits them, placing a non-executable read-only segment first. The
latter is the default on x86, partly to avoid making the ELF headers
executable when mappings start from the beginning of the file.

Other architectures tend to default to -z noseparate-code, which
makes it more likely that the text segment is the first PT_LOAD.

LLVM lld behaves differently again: --rosegment (equivalent to
-z separate-code) is enabled by default on all architectures, which
similarly tends to place the executable segment after an initial
read-only one. That default is clearly less friendly to large-page
text mappings.

Thanks,
Rui

2026-03-14.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues with contpte-sized exec folios on arm64, specifically for 16K and 64K page configurations. The first issue is that the current implementation returns an incorrect order for these page sizes, which is fixed by using a different calculation. The second issue is that readahead is disabled after 100 page faults due to the mmap_miss heuristic, but this only happens on 64K pages because the decrement paths are inactive. This is fixed by moving the VM_EXEC readahead block above the mmap_miss check. Finally, the patch ensures that the virtual address is aligned to CONT_PTE_SIZE (2M) for contpte mapping, which requires alignment of the VMA start address.
